heroOverview: Hypernasality is a resonance disorder in which too much sound energy passes through the nasal cavity during speech, giving the voice an excessively nasal quality on oral sounds such as vowels and oral consonants. It occurs when the velopharyngeal valve — the soft palate and surrounding pharyngeal muscles — fails to close the connection between the oral and nasal cavities during speech. The leading cause is velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI), most commonly resulting from a cleft palate or submucous cleft palate, which affects approximately 1 in 700 live births worldwide. Hypernasality can also arise following adenoidectomy, neurological injury, or as a component of various syndromes including 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) syndrome. When untreated, hypernasality significantly impairs speech intelligibility and social communication; with appropriate surgical and speech therapy intervention, the large majority of affected individuals achieve normal or near-normal resonance.

keyStats.prevalence: Hypernasality as a consequence of cleft palate affects approximately 1 in 700 newborns; VPI from all causes affects an estimated 10–15% of children with repaired cleft palate
keyStats.demographics: Most commonly diagnosed in young children with cleft palate or syndrome-associated VPI; adenoidectomy-related hypernasality affects approximately 1 in 1,500 adenoidectomy patients
keyStats.avgAge: Usually identified by age 2–4 years during speech development evaluation; post-adenoidectomy onset is in childhood and adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: Cleft palate occurs in approximately 175,000 births worldwide annually (WHO global burden data)

definition: Hypernasality (ICD-10: R49.21) is a disorder of speech resonance characterised by excessive nasal airflow and acoustic coupling during the production of oral speech sounds. Normally, the velopharyngeal mechanism — comprising the velum (soft palate), superior and lateral pharyngeal walls, and associated musculature — closes completely during oral speech, directing sound energy through the oral cavity. When this closure is incomplete, air and sound escape into the nasal cavity and produce the perceptual quality of hypernasality on vowels, pressure consonants, and other oral phonemes. Velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) is the structural and functional failure of this closure and is the umbrella term covering velopharyngeal incompetence (neuromotor), velopharyngeal inadequacy (mixed), and velopharyngeal mislearning (articulation error without structural deficiency). The most common cause is cleft palate, either overt or submucous, but VPI can follow adenoidectomy (removing the adenoid that was compensatorily filling the pharynx), trauma, neurological conditions such as cerebral palsy, or occur as part of genetic syndromes. Hypernasality must be distinguished from hyponasality (insufficient nasal resonance), mixed resonance disorder, and culde-sac resonance (sound trapped in the nasal cavity without egress). The condition is managed by a multidisciplinary craniofacial or cleft team including speech-language pathology, otolaryngology, and plastic or craniofacial surgery.

typesClassification.description: Insufficient tissue to achieve complete velopharyngeal closure during speech, most commonly due to cleft palate, submucous cleft, or post-adenoidectomy, where the velum is physically too short relative to the pharynx.

The tissue is anatomically sufficient but motor control is impaired due to neurological conditions such as cerebral palsy, dysarthria, or lower cranial nerve palsy, preventing complete or consistent closure.

Part of a genetic or chromosomal syndrome; 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge/velocardiofacial) is the most common genetic cause of VPI, with hypernasality present in 75–100% of affected individuals.

Following removal of the adenoids in a child with a pre-existing borderline short velum or submucous cleft, the adenoid pad that was compensatorily aiding closure is lost; onset is typically weeks to months post-surgery.
Speech patterns producing nasal airflow that mimic VPI but arise from learned articulation errors rather than structural or neurological deficiency; responds to speech therapy alone without surgery.

primarySymptoms: Excessively nasal voice quality on vowels and oral consonants, described by listeners as 'talking through the nose', which is the defining perceptual characteristic.
Audible nasal air emission — a hissing or snorting sound during pressure consonants such as /p/, /b/, /t/, /d/, /k/, /g/, /s/ — caused by air escaping through the incompletely closed velopharynx.
Reduced speech pressure, meaning plosives and fricatives that require oral air pressure (such as /p/, /s/, /f/) sound weak or distorted because pressure escapes nasally before it can build up.
Nasal grimacing — visible wrinkling or flaring of the nostrils as the speaker tries to increase nasal resistance, a compensatory behaviour.
Compensatory articulation errors such as glottal stops replacing plosives or pharyngeal fricatives replacing sibilants, which develop when the speaker cannot generate adequate oral pressure.
Reduced speech intelligibility, particularly for connected speech and in background noise, when several phoneme categories are affected.
Frequent nasal regurgitation of liquids in infants and young children with unrepaired or recently repaired cleft palate, indicating failed velopharyngeal seal during swallowing.
Hoarse or hypophonic voice secondary to hyperfunctional laryngeal compensation when the speaker increases laryngeal effort to compensate for nasal air loss.
Difficulty with certain activities requiring oral pressure — such as blowing, whistling, playing wind instruments, or using a straw — which require velopharyngeal closure.
Psychosocial difficulties including reduced confidence, avoidance of speaking situations, and bullying in school-aged children when hypernasality is conspicuous.
earlyWarningSigns: A child's vowel sounds beginning to have an excessively nasal quality at 18–24 months, when connected speech is developing.
Visible nasal air emission or nasal grimacing when a child attempts pressure consonants.
Failure to improve nasal resonance following cleft palate repair, suggesting persistent or residual VPI.
New onset of nasal voice in a child after adenoidectomy — a recognised complication requiring assessment.
emergencySigns: Sudden new hypernasality with facial weakness, dysphagia, or other cranial nerve signs pointing to an acute neurological event (stroke, bulbar palsy) requiring emergency assessment.
Severe nasal regurgitation in an infant with feeding failure and poor weight gain, which requires urgent craniofacial team review.
Hypernasality developing after head or neck trauma, which may indicate injury to velopharyngeal structures.

causes.description: The most common cause: structural disruption of the velum prevents normal muscular closure even after surgical repair in 10–30% of cases, depending on the cleft type, repair technique, and timing. Submucous cleft palate is a concealed variant in which the palate surface is intact but the underlying musculature is abnormal, causing VPI that often presents later during speech development.

This chromosomal microdeletion causes VPI through a combination of short velum, hypotonic velopharyngeal musculature, and aberrant internal carotid artery position that constrains surgical options; hypernasality is present in 75–100% of affected individuals and may persist despite multiple surgeries.

In children with a short velum or subclinical submucous cleft, the adenoid pad provides compensatory posterior pharyngeal tissue that aids velopharyngeal contact. Removal of the adenoids exposes the pre-existing insufficiency, producing hypernasality that is typically detected weeks to months post-operatively.

Cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury, stroke, motor neuron disease, and myasthenia gravis can all impair the fine motor control required for consistent velopharyngeal closure, producing hypernasality as part of a wider dysarthria or bulbar syndrome.

causes.cause: Trauma or surgery to the velopharynx
causes.description: Direct trauma to the palate, or surgery for palatal tumours, can disrupt muscular continuity and produce iatrogenic VPI; post-radiation fibrosis in the nasopharynx can also impair closure.

causes.cause: Idiopathic or isolated VPI without identified cause
causes.description: Some individuals have a structurally short or underpowered velum without an identifiable syndrome, congenital anomaly, or neurological cause; the exact prevalence is unclear but recognised clinically.

riskFactors.factor: Cleft palate or submucous cleft palate
riskFactors.description: The strongest and most common risk factor for hypernasality; the type and extent of the cleft, the age and technique of repair, and the presence of a submucous component all influence the likelihood of residual VPI after surgery.

This chromosomal microdeletion is present in approximately 8% of non-syndromic cleft palate cases and is the most common genetic cause of VPI in non-cleft individuals; testing is recommended in all children with VPI without an obvious structural cause.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of cleft palate or VPI
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of an index case have a 3–5% recurrence risk of cleft palate, carrying associated VPI risk.

Pre-operative speech assessment and nasopharyngoscopy in borderline cases can identify children at risk of post-adenoidectomy hypernasality; some guidelines recommend conservative management of adenoid hypertrophy in this group.
Conditions causing generalised hypotonia, bulbar palsy, or upper motor neuron damage increase the risk of velopharyngeal incompetence; hypernasality in this context is part of a broader dysarthria.
Premature infants have higher rates of neuromotor problems, including hypotonia of the velopharyngeal muscles, contributing to resonance disorders during speech development.

affectedDemographics: Infants and young children with cleft palate, with or without cleft lip
Children and adolescents with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome
Children who have undergone adenoidectomy and have a pre-existing borderline velopharyngeal mechanism
Individuals with cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury, or bulbar neurological conditions
Adults following head and neck cancer treatment affecting the nasopharynx or palate
Anyone with a family history of cleft palate or known velopharyngeal insufficiency
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is made through perceptual speech assessment by a speech-language pathologist trained in resonance disorders, supplemented by instrumental evaluation. The perceptual assessment uses standardised protocols to rate the degree of hypernasality on a scale, identify compensatory articulation errors, and characterise the pattern of nasal air emission. Nasometry provides an objective measurement of nasalance — the acoustic ratio of nasal to total sound energy — with values above approximately 32% (on English speech samples) supporting a diagnosis of hypernasality. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy, performed through the nose to visualise the velopharyngeal port from above during speech, is the gold standard for defining the size, pattern, and symmetry of the VPI, which directly informs surgical planning. Multiview videofluoroscopy assesses velopharyngeal movement during sustained phonemes and connected speech in the coronal, lateral, and base views, providing dynamic information that complements endoscopy. In all children with VPI without a clear structural cause, chromosomal microarray testing for 22q11.2 deletion is recommended, as it identifies a syndrome with important cardiac, immune, and endocrine implications beyond the speech problem. Hearing testing is essential because middle ear dysfunction (common with cleft palate) can affect speech production and monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Trained speech-language pathologist listens to speech and rates hypernasality severity, nasal air emission, and articulation errors using validated scales to establish the clinical impact and treatment needs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You or your child will be asked to repeat words, sentences, and connected speech samples; the clinician may use a mirror under the nose to detect nasal air emission. The session takes 30–60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures nasalance — the proportion of acoustic energy emitted from the nose — to provide an objective, reproducible resonance measurement and track treatment progress.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small paddle with acoustic sensors is placed between the nose and mouth while you speak into a microphone; the device is non-invasive and takes about 15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the velopharyngeal port during speech to determine the size, location, and pattern of closure gap, guiding surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible endoscope is passed through the nose after topical anaesthetic spray; you will be asked to speak, sustain sounds, and sing while the clinician visualises closure. The procedure takes 10–15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Dynamic X-ray assessment of velopharyngeal movement during speech in multiple planes, complementing nasopharyngoscopy and providing lateral-view closure depth measurement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Barium contrast is used as a marker in the nasopharynx while X-ray images are taken during speech; radiation exposure is low and the session takes about 20–30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies 22q11.2 deletion syndrome in children with VPI of unclear cause, enabling diagnosis of a condition with significant cardiac, immune, and developmental implications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood sample is taken; results typically return within 2–4 weeks from a genetics laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies conductive hearing loss from middle ear dysfunction — common in children with cleft palate — which affects speech development and monitoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Behavioural hearing tests and a tympanogram (pressure measurement in the ear canal) are performed; testing takes 30–60 minutes depending on the child's age.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hypernasality is directed by the cause, severity, and speech-language pattern identified during multidisciplinary assessment. When VPI is mild and predominantly a matter of learned articulation errors (velopharyngeal mislearning), intensive speech therapy alone can resolve hypernasality without surgery in a majority of cases. When structural or significant neuromotor VPI is confirmed, surgery is the primary intervention and speech therapy provides pre- and post-surgical support. The most widely used surgical procedures are pharyngeal flap surgery and sphincter pharyngoplasty, both of which narrow the velopharyngeal port surgically to enable closure; palate re-repair (Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty revision) is preferred when the velum is of adequate length but has an abnormal muscle orientation, as in submucous cleft. Prosthetic management with a palatal lift or speech bulb appliance is used in patients who are not surgical candidates — often those with severe neurological disease or those awaiting surgery. Post-surgical speech therapy is essential to retrain velopharyngeal movement patterns and eliminate compensatory articulation errors that persist after structural correction. Children with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome may require specialised surgical planning because of medially placed internal carotid arteries, and their VPI sometimes persists despite surgery, requiring more than one procedure. Treatment should be completed ideally before school age (by age 4–5) to maximise the window for optimal speech development, though adolescents and adults also benefit significantly from surgery and therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Intensive speech therapy for velopharyngeal mislearning
medicalTreatments.description: When hypernasality arises from learned nasal articulation errors rather than structural deficiency, a structured programme targeting oral placement, oral air pressure, and elimination of nasal substitutions resolves the problem in the majority of cases without surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of hypernasality in approximately 70–80% of confirmed velopharyngeal mislearning cases with intensive therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Prosthetic palatal lift appliance
medicalTreatments.description: A dental device worn in the mouth that mechanically lifts the velum toward the pharynx, reducing the functional gap; used in neuromotor VPI when surgery is contraindicated or as a diagnostic trial to predict surgical benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Nasalance reduction and perceptual improvement in approximately 50–70% of selected neuromotor VPI patients; gains may not be permanent without sustained use.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech bulb obturator
medicalTreatments.description: A prosthetic appliance with a bulb extension that occludes the velopharyngeal port; used in children and adults with significant VPI who are not surgical candidates, particularly in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome with medially displaced carotid arteries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant resonance improvement in approximately 60–75% of users; requires regular orthodontic review and replacement.

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-surgical and post-surgical speech therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Speech therapy before surgery prepares the patient for post-operative resonance changes and helps eliminate compensatory articulation errors; post-surgical therapy retrains normal velopharyngeal movement patterns and consolidates speech gains.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined surgery and therapy achieves normal or near-normal resonance in 75–90% of primary VPI cases compared to surgery alone.

surgicalOptions.name: Pharyngeal flap surgery
surgicalOptions.description: A midline flap of posterior pharyngeal wall tissue is raised and inset into the posterior velum, creating a central bridge that narrows the velopharyngeal port into two lateral ports; lateral ports can be closed during phonation by the lateral pharyngeal walls. Most commonly used for central or symmetric VPI gaps.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Normal or near-normal resonance in approximately 70–85% of selected cases; risk of hyponasality or obstructive sleep apnoea from an overly wide flap.

surgicalOptions.name: Sphincter pharyngoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Bilateral superiorly based flaps of posterior tonsillar pillar muscle are raised and inset into the posterior pharyngeal wall at the level of attempted closure, creating a dynamic sphincter that reduces the gap size; preferred for lateral or coronal VPI patterns.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Normal or near-normal resonance in 70–85% of patients; similar risks of hyponasality and sleep-disordered breathing as pharyngeal flap.

surgicalOptions.name: Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty revision (re-repair)
surgicalOptions.description: A secondary palate repair technique that repositions the levator veli palatini muscle into a more transverse orientation and lengthens the velum by interlocking Z-plasties; preferred when the primary repair left the muscle in a poor orientation, as in submucous cleft palate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Eliminates or greatly reduces VPI in approximately 65–80% of suitable cases, with lower hyponasality risk than pharyngeal flap.

alternativeTreatments.name: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) biofeedback training
alternativeTreatments.description: CPAP applied to the nose during speech exercises creates resistance that increases velopharyngeal muscle activity; used as an adjunct speech therapy tool in selected mild VPI cases to strengthen closure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback using nasometry
alternativeTreatments.description: Real-time nasometry display during speech therapy gives the speaker immediate feedback on nasalance levels, enabling self-correction; effective as an adjunct in mislearning cases and post-surgical therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Hypernasality should be assessed by a multidisciplinary cleft or craniofacial team including a speech-language pathologist with resonance training, an otolaryngologist, and a plastic or craniofacial surgeon. Early referral — ideally by age 3 — allows surgery to be planned and completed before school entry, maximising speech development outcomes. Children with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome should also be seen by genetics, cardiology, and immunology.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a centre with a designated cleft or craniofacial team that includes experienced resonance speech-language pathologists, a surgeon with a specific practice in VPI surgery, and access to nasopharyngoscopy and nasometry. Ask how many VPI surgeries the surgeon performs annually and what the team's rates of normal resonance post-surgery are. Avoid centres that do not offer nasopharyngoscopy as part of pre-surgical planning.
preventionStrategies: Screen for submucous cleft palate in all children referred for adenoidectomy, using nasopharyngoscopy and palatal examination, to identify those at risk of post-adenoidectomy hypernasality.
Test for 22q11.2 deletion in any child with VPI without a clear structural explanation, enabling comprehensive syndrome management.
Refer all children with repaired cleft palate for regular speech surveillance, since residual VPI can be identified and treated early.
Complete primary cleft palate repair at the recommended timing (typically 6–18 months) to optimise velopharyngeal function before speech development begins.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled speech therapy and cleft team appointments consistently — missed sessions slow treatment progress significantly.
Practise speech therapy exercises daily as prescribed, using real-time feedback tools if available.
Use amplification in noisy environments to compensate for reduced speech intelligibility while awaiting or recovering from surgery.
Encourage a child with hypernasality to continue participating fully in social and educational settings, with school support if intelligibility is significantly impaired.

recommended: Normal diet for most patients; no specific dietary restrictions relate to hypernasality
Adequate hydration to maintain mucosal health
Post-surgical soft diet for 2–4 weeks after pharyngoplasty or palate repair
avoid: Hard, sharp, or abrasive foods for several weeks following palatal surgery to protect healing mucosa
Carbonated drinks and straws immediately after palatal surgery

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions for most patients with hypernasality. Following velopharyngeal surgery, strenuous physical activity is restricted for approximately 2–4 weeks to allow mucosal healing. Speech therapy exercises are the most relevant 'exercise' — daily consistent practice during the post-surgical window is associated with better outcomes.
dailyManagement: Complete daily speech therapy exercises prescribed by your speech-language pathologist.
Use a mirror or nasometry app to monitor nasal air emission during practice if these tools have been recommended.
Attend all post-surgical follow-up appointments — resonance can change in the months after surgery as swelling resolves.
Seek prompt assessment if new snoring, mouth breathing, or obstructive sleep symptoms develop after pharyngoplasty.
Support school communication with a teacher explanation if intelligibility is significantly affected.
prognosis: The prognosis for hypernasality is good to excellent with timely and appropriate treatment. Children with VPI from repaired cleft palate who receive surgery and speech therapy achieve normal or near-normal resonance in 75–90% of cases. When surgery must be repeated (as in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome), outcomes are somewhat lower but still meaningful improvement is achieved in the majority. Velopharyngeal mislearning without structural VPI responds well to speech therapy alone in approximately 70–80% of cases. Post-adenoidectomy hypernasality resolves spontaneously within 3–6 months in the majority of cases without a pre-existing cleft or submucous cleft; those with an underlying VPI require speech therapy or surgery. Adults treated after years of compensatory speech often need longer therapy to retrain ingrained error patterns, but surgical benefit is maintained and intelligibility typically improves significantly.
recoveryTimeline: Post-surgical recovery from pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty involves 1–2 weeks of soft diet, 2–4 weeks of activity restriction, and 3–6 months for full resonance to stabilise as post-operative swelling resolves. Speech therapy typically resumes 4–6 weeks post-surgery. Velopharyngeal mislearning cases may see substantial progress within 12–20 sessions of intensive therapy over 3–6 months.
complications: Hyponasality and nasal obstruction — the most common surgical complication, resulting from an overly wide pharyngeal flap or sphincter that over-corrects the VPI.
Obstructive sleep apnoea — partial nasopharyngeal obstruction from pharyngoplasty can cause snoring and sleep-disordered breathing requiring post-operative monitoring with overnight oximetry.
Persistent hypernasality despite surgery, particularly in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, sometimes requiring revision surgery.
Velopharyngeal scarring and fistula formation after primary or revision palate repair, contributing to ongoing resonance and articulation difficulties.

Social and psychological impact — conspicuous hypernasality can cause significant peer difficulties, reduced confidence, and avoidance of speaking that requires targeted support alongside speech treatment.

supportResources.name: Cleft Lip and Palate Association (CLAPA)
supportResources.description: UK support organisation for people affected by cleft lip and palate, providing information, peer support, and referral guidance.

supportResources.name: American Cleft Palate–Craniofacial Association (ACPA)
supportResources.description: Professional and patient resources for cleft and craniofacial care, including a team directory and family information on VPI.

supportResources.name: NIDCD — Cleft Palate and Speech
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of how cleft palate and VPI affect speech from the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders.

sources.title: Kummer AW. Cleft Palate and Craniofacial Conditions: A Comprehensive Guide (4th ed). Jones & Bartlett, 2020
NICE — Cleft lip and/or palate: assessment and management (NG17)
Perkins JA et al. Management of velopharyngeal insufficiency. Otolaryngologic Clinics of North America, 2012
McDonald-McGinn DM et al. 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. Nature Reviews Disease Primers, 2015
Sell D et al. Cleft audit protocol for speech — augmented (CAPS-A). Cleft Palate-Craniofacial Journal, 2009

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyponasality
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyponasality means too little nasal resonance — the voice sounds blocked or de-nasalised; hypernasality means too much nasal resonance. They are opposite resonance disorders with different causes and treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dysarthria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dysarthria is a broad motor speech disorder affecting articulation, rate, and prosody; hypernasality from neuromotor VPI is one component of dysarthria but does not define it. The distinction matters for treatment planning.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasal polyps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nasal polyps obstruct the nasal passage and cause hyponasality, not hypernasality. They are sometimes confused because both affect nasal resonance, but the direction of resonance change is opposite.

relatedConditions.name: Hyponasality
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite resonance disorder — both arise from abnormal velopharyngeal or nasal function and are assessed by the same clinical team.

relatedConditions.name: Dysarthria and anarthria
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypernasality from neuromotor VPI is a component of many dysarthria types; the conditions co-occur in cerebral palsy, bulbar palsy, and other neurological conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Dysphonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Voice quality disorders and resonance disorders often co-occur, particularly when hyperfunctional laryngeal compensation for VPI produces secondary dysphonia.

faqs.question: What does hypernasality sound like?
faqs.answer: Hypernasality makes the voice sound excessively nasal — as if the speaker is 'talking through the nose'. It is most obvious on vowels and oral consonants. In severe cases, pressure consonants such as /p/, /b/, /s/ sound weak or are accompanied by audible nasal air escaping through the nostrils.

faqs.question: What causes hypernasality?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) — the soft palate fails to close the passage between the mouth and nose during speech. This most often results from cleft palate, submucous cleft, removal of the adenoids (adenoidectomy), or 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. Neurological conditions such as cerebral palsy can also cause it.

faqs.question: Can hypernasality be corrected?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most cases of hypernasality are treated successfully with surgery, speech therapy, or both. Velopharyngeal mislearning (learned nasal speech without structural deficiency) responds to speech therapy alone in about 70–80% of cases. Structural VPI is corrected surgically — pharyngeal flap or sphincter pharyngoplasty — achieving normal resonance in 75–90% of cases.

faqs.question: Is hypernasality always due to cleft palate?
faqs.answer: No. Cleft palate is the most common cause, but hypernasality also occurs with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (even without a cleft), after adenoidectomy, in neurological conditions such as cerebral palsy, and from learned articulation patterns (velopharyngeal mislearning) without any structural problem.

faqs.question: When should a child with hypernasality be referred?
faqs.answer: Referral to a speech-language pathologist with resonance training should happen as soon as persistent nasal voice quality is noticed — typically around age 3 if it has not improved after palate repair, or at any age if newly noticed. Early referral allows treatment before school entry and optimises speech development.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypernasality and hyponasality?
faqs.answer: Hypernasality means too much nasal resonance — the voice sounds excessively nasal. Hyponasality means too little — the voice sounds blocked, as in a severe head cold. They are opposite disorders: hypernasality comes from too much airflow through the nose during speech; hyponasality comes from nasal obstruction preventing normal nasal resonance on nasal sounds.

faqs.question: Can adults develop hypernasality?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults can develop hypernasality following neurological events such as stroke or brain injury affecting velopharyngeal motor control, after surgery to the nasopharynx or palate, or as part of progressive neuromuscular conditions. Adult-onset hypernasality requires laryngological and neurological assessment.

faqs.question: What is velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI)?
faqs.answer: VPI is the umbrella term for failure of the velopharyngeal mechanism — the soft palate and surrounding throat muscles — to close the passage between the mouth and nose during speech. It causes hypernasality and nasal air emission. VPI can be structural (tissue is insufficient), neuromotor (muscle control is impaired), or learned (mislearning without structural cause).

faqs.question: What is pharyngeal flap surgery?
faqs.answer: Pharyngeal flap surgery narrows the gap between the soft palate and the back wall of the throat by creating a tissue bridge. This reduces nasal air escape during speech. It is the most commonly used surgery for VPI and achieves normal resonance in about 70–85% of cases. A risk is over-correction causing nasal obstruction or sleep apnoea.

faqs.question: What is sphincter pharyngoplasty?
faqs.answer: Sphincter pharyngoplasty creates a muscular ring (sphincter) at the back of the throat by inset muscle flaps from the side throat walls, narrowing the velopharyngeal port. It is preferred for wide lateral VPI gaps and achieves similar success rates to pharyngeal flap (70–85% normal resonance). Both surgeries carry a risk of hyponasality from over-narrowing.

faqs.question: What is 22q11.2 deletion syndrome and why does it cause hypernasality?
faqs.answer: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (also called DiGeorge or velocardiofacial syndrome) is a chromosomal condition caused by a tiny deletion on chromosome 22. It causes a short, underpowered soft palate and sometimes medially displaced carotid arteries. VPI with hypernasality occurs in 75–100% of affected individuals and may need multiple surgeries. The syndrome also affects the heart, immune system, and calcium metabolism.

faqs.question: Can adenoid removal cause a nasal voice?
faqs.answer: Yes. In children who have a borderline short soft palate or a subclinical submucous cleft, the adenoid pad helps the palate close the nasal passage. After adenoidectomy, that compensatory tissue is gone and hypernasality appears — usually within weeks to months. A pre-operative speech check in children with suspected palate abnormalities can identify those at risk.

faqs.question: How is hypernasality measured objectively?
faqs.answer: Nasometry measures nasalance — the proportion of acoustic energy from the nose versus total sound output during speech. Values above about 32% on standard English speech samples support hypernasality. Nasometry is non-invasive, takes 15 minutes, and is used to quantify severity, guide treatment decisions, and track progress after therapy or surgery.

faqs.question: Does hypernasality affect intelligibility?
faqs.answer: Yes, in moderate to severe cases. Hypernasality distorts pressure consonants (such as p, b, t, d, k, g, s, f) because air pressure escapes nasally rather than building up in the mouth. Combined with compensatory articulation errors that some speakers develop, this can substantially reduce how clearly the speaker can be understood, particularly in noisy environments.

faqs.question: What tests are done before VPI surgery?
faqs.answer: Nasopharyngoscopy — a flexible camera through the nose to view the velopharyngeal port during speech — is the key pre-surgical test; it defines the gap size and pattern that determines which surgical approach is best. Nasometry provides objective resonance measurement. In unexplained VPI, chromosome microarray testing for 22q11.2 deletion is also performed.

faqs.question: Is hypernasality a learning disability?
faqs.answer: No. Hypernasality is a speech resonance disorder caused by structural or neuromotor problems with the velopharyngeal mechanism. It does not directly affect cognition or learning ability, though its social impact and communication difficulties can affect a child's confidence and school participation if not treated.

faqs.question: What happens at a cleft team appointment for hypernasality?
faqs.answer: A multidisciplinary team assessment typically includes a speech-language pathologist who rates resonance and articulation, a surgeon who reviews anatomy and surgical options, and sometimes a psychologist and audiologist. Together they agree on whether speech therapy alone, surgery, or a combined approach is recommended.

faqs.question: Can hypernasality come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Hypernasality can recur after surgery, particularly in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome where the underlying mechanism (short velum and hypotonic musculature) tends to undermine surgical corrections. About 10–30% of surgically treated VPI cases require revision surgery. Post-surgical speech therapy is important to consolidate gains and detect early recurrence.

metaDescription: Hypernasality makes the voice excessively nasal due to velopharyngeal insufficiency. Learn about cleft palate causes, VPI surgery and speech therapy options.
